turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 16-5294, McWilliams versus Dunn, Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, this is a little longer, but McWilliams versus Dunn involves a petitioner, James Edmund McWilliams, Jr. He was convicted of capital murder 31 years ago by an Alabama jury and he was sentenced to death. He challenged his sentence on appeal, arguing that the state had failed to provide him with the expert mental health assistance that the Constitution requires. This case asked us to consider, and we do consider, it is a habeas corpus case, whether the Alabama court's refusal was “Contrary to or involved in unreasonable application of clearly established federal law.” So, what was the federal law at that time, clearly established? We hold that it did violate that standard. In Ake v. Oklahoma, a decision that was -- had been made before the -- they made their decision in – in this case, it clearly established that when certain threshold criteria are met, the state must provide an indigent defendant with access to a mental health expert who is sufficiently available to the defense and independent from the prosecution to effectively “Conduct an appropriate examination and assist in evaluation, preparation, and presentation of the defense.” Petitioner in this case did not receive that assistance. The Alabama Court of Criminal Appeals reasoned that the State met Ake's requirements by arranging for McWilliams, the defendant, to be examined by a psychologist, Dr. John Goff, who performed certain mental health tests that McWilliams requested. Now, that reasoning of the Court is incorrect. It is not enough. Ake does not just require an examination. It requires the state to provide the defense with “Access to a competent psychiatrist who will conduct an appropriate examination and assist in evaluation, preparation, and presentation of the defense.” We will assume that Alabama met the examination part of this requirement by providing for Dr. Goff's examination of McWilliams. But we do not see how the state satisfied the other three parts. Neither Dr. Goff nor any other expert helped the defense evaluate and understand McWilliams' extensive medical records. Neither Dr. Goff nor any other expert helped the defense prepare and present legal arguments based on that information. Neither Dr. Goff nor any other expert helped the defense prepare or -- direct or cross-examination of any witness, or presented testimony himself. The trial court did not ask Dr. Goff or anyone else to provide the defense with help in evaluating, preparing, and presenting its case. And when McWilliams asked for the additional assistance, to which he was constitutionally entitled under Ake, at his judicial sentencing hearing, the trial court rebuffs that request. Because Alabama's provision of mental health assistance fell short of what Ake requires, we conclude that the Alabama Court decision affirming McWilliams' conviction and sentence was “Contrary to or involved in unreasonable application of clearly established federal law.” The decision of the Court of Appeals of -- is reversed. The Court of Appeals is reversed. The case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Alito has written a dissent which the Chief Justice and Justices Thomas and Gorsuch have joined.
